Peter closes his letter by fixing his eyes on the weight of leadership and the reality of suffering that frames the Christian life. He speaks as one who has walked this path himself—a witness to Christ's sufferings, a man who will soon face his own. When he calls elders to shepherd God's flock willingly and without greed, he is not offering them a position of power but a call to sacrifice that mirrors Christ's own care for His people. The shepherd does not lead from a distance or lord authority over those entrusted to him; he becomes an example, offering his life as the measure of his teaching. This is not management. This is costly love. And Peter reminds these leaders that their work will not go unnoticed—when the Chief Shepherd appears, they will receive a glory that cannot fade. But that promise is inseparable from the suffering that comes before it.

The letter then pivots to address the whole church, and here Peter cuts through to something we desperately need to hear. He does not promise comfort or safety. Instead, he calls us to humility before God's mighty hand, knowing that pride is the posture God opposes. He tells us to cast our anxiety on Him—not because anxiety will vanish, but because He cares for us in the midst of it. Then comes the shock: be alert, because your enemy the devil prowls like a roaring lion. This is not theoretical. Peter is writing to believers scattered throughout regions where their faith was costing them everything. They needed to know that their suffering was real, their enemy was real, and their resistance mattered. Yet he does not leave them there. He reminds them that brothers throughout the world suffer the same things—they are not alone—and more importantly, that God Himself will restore, secure, strengthen, and establish them after they have suffered for a little while.

That phrase—"a little while"—deserves to stop us. In the midst of real persecution, real loss, real pain, Peter calls the duration brief. Not because suffering is insignificant, but because eternity is that significant. God has called His people to eternal glory in Christ, and everything that happens before that is temporary by comparison. Peter ends by anchoring this entire letter in God's power and grace, reminding his readers that they stand firm in "the true grace of God." This is the ground beneath your feet when everything else shakes—not your strength, not your circumstances, but God's grace revealed in Christ, who suffered before you and reigns above you now. The question Peter leaves us with is whether we will trust that grace enough to obey, to lead humbly, to submit, to resist evil, and to endure.
